I'm good.

Thanks for you.
It's nice.

Is it one of those classic, this week has been one of the busiest because you're on vacation next week, or are you feeling good?
That is an understatement.
The last few weeks have been crazy.
We just had a big deliverable this morning that I presented, so that's a breath of fresh air, and now it's closing up.
Good to get that out of the way.
Okay.
Yeah.
For sure.
For sure.
Well, hopefully when you come back also, it is not too bad, but I was on my own vacation.
Man, was that a month ago now?
That's crazy.
Something like that.
But yeah, the week before and the week after were super, super busy, but definitely easier than the previous vacation I took.
go go back to
I think as long as you're able to make some of that progress, you know, that's good.

Have you heard of Banff in Alberta, Canada?
I've never been, but yeah, I've heard.

And so they talk about Banff pretty frequently as like a getaway spot.
Beautiful.
It's beautiful.
The company I work at now is located in Calgary.
So I've gone up for a work trip, and I took a day tour around Banff, and I'm like, I got to get back here.
So going for five days, going to tour all the lakes and whatnot, do some hiking and stuff.
It's cool.
Very cool.
Nice.
Where are you at now?
Where are you based?
I'm based out of Temecula, California.
So if you're not familiar with Southern California, it's about an hour north of San Diego.
Okay.
I know I've heard the name, but yeah, would not have been able to place that on a map, even though I am in California.
You're Northern California, right?
Yeah.
Yeah, we're the competition in Napa Valley.
That's probably how Temeculous is the best known.
Okay, interesting.
A lot of wineries.
Okay.
That's cool.
That's cool.
Nice.
Well, I you've already chatted with a few folks.
You know, I have a handful of questions to go through today.
Before I jump into that, did they share any background on me or would be helpful to give you?
I'd love to share a little bit of background on you.
I know it was originally scheduled to meet with Andy.
So I know they threw you into the ring at the last hour.
So I'd love to get an idea of what you do at GrowthX and just kind of the lowdown.
Okay, cool.
Where to start?
So I've worn a bunch of different hats here.
I was the first customer.
I signed up in, I don't know, April or June of last year or something like that.
I can't quite recall, but...
I don't No respect.
respect.
Oh, yeah.
Previous to this, was leading growth at a variety of different stops.
A lot of times companies would hire me because they're trying to diversify their channels, not be as reliant on paid, things like that.
I was leading growth at this company called Homebase, met Marcel when he was doing these workshops, and signed up to be a customer soon after.
Then I joined back in October.
I was leading the delivery side for a good chunk of my tenure, I don't know, something like October through April or May of this year.
So, you know, onboarded a lot of our clients, the team, et cetera.
And then we had a need for helping out on the sales side, getting Marcel out of the weeds of the day-to-day, doing founder-led sales, and so spent a few months on that end of things.
Then we have our first AE who started recently and is doing amazing.
So now back on some of the delivery side, helping out some of our custom engagements, helping cover...

For someone who had departed on the director's side, so kind of coming back with a somewhat fresh perspective on the delivery side of the house, but also having all that context from, you know, seeing the journey from all these different angles.
And so definitely been a fun challenge, but yeah, happy to get into questions towards the tail end, too, and share a little bit of context about me.
Amazing.
That sounds great.
Yeah, and I guess I'll kind of give you the lowdown of myself.
So Matt Ressler, I'm currently at an Omnicom agency, so really in the weeds with enterprise strategy from a holistic perspective, so organic paid and the AI AEO search.
We just won the U.S.
Search Award for 2025 Best Use of Search in Finance, so that's a campaign that I just led with City National Bank, one of the clients here.
I'm sure we'll talk about that in a few.

used to work with Kirkland years ago.
to office.

But really piqued my interest because your model is really unique because you kind of craft some software that you integrate with your clients.
And it's a lot more intricate, and I think you're the big leader in the industry.
So yeah, so I've been doing the organic search stuff for quite a while now.

touched anything from small business to medium size in the last few years has really been this enterprise strategy across a couple different verticals, finance, retail, and vet slash healthcare have been the main three.
But yeah, that's like the quick elevator pitch on me.
Cool.
Yeah, no, I appreciate the background.
Yeah, definitely curious to understand both on the AEO, I almost said GE.
very
You I'm trying, we're in plenty of like, you want to call it AEO, GEO, who knows, whatever.
I feel like now we're starting to use AEO more again, but it feels like a little unnatural because we were using GEO for a while.

I honestly don't even care, but I'm sick of saying AEO slash GEO, so I just want to pick one, but anyway, so some of the questions, you know, definitely curious on like both sides of that a bit, but yeah, I have a handful of questions to go through.
And then want to make sure I give you, you know, something like 10 minutes for questions at the end as well.
So let's dive into it.

So you were, you were mentioning strategy, you know, as you're thinking about building a strategy, like how are you thinking about balancing both like the short-term and the long-term as you're developing long-term, short-term, long-term wins as you're thinking about developing that strategy?
Yeah, short-term wins are interesting and it depends on the client.
City National Bank, I mentioned, is very different than one of the other clients like Delta Faucet.
They're both in very different environments in their industry.
City National is a much smaller player in a very regulated and competitive environment where the Bank of America and Wells Fargo's of the world really dominate that.
So short term for them, it's getting them cited within AI as much as possible and going for very specific queries.
So the campaign that we won the award on was related to mortgages.
And our strategy there was to target specifically on California homebuyers because they have such a dominant presence in Los Angeles and the Bay Area.
And they're offering a home grant.
So we built out a content library that targeted very specific.
Hey, I'm a first time homebuyer.
I'm looking to buy.
These are questions that I have about the LA area or the Bay Area.
Is there any opportunity for me to get money for down payments, et cetera?
So very long tail, very specific things that they might ask.
CheckTBT.
CheckTBT.
We got a bunch of citations and really great rankings.
We grew that to about 1100 form fills over the course of a year.
So they went from 200 mortgage contacts to 1100 year over year, just based off of that alone.
And then on Delta Faucet, on the other side, their short term strategy is different.
Their SEO is not very good, but they have authority behind their name.
So let's just get you up for anything possible.

But long term, it's just building that exercise of using good EAT, having high quality content, answering the right questions, tracking that, testing different optimizations, and then revisiting those every couple months to see if things are moving in the right direction.
So yeah, that short term strategy, little different depending on the client, but ultimately it's a regular long term strategy.
Got it.
Got it.
Helpful.
Can you share a little bit more, just like an example, maybe it's related to that or separate?
Were you adapted quickly to changing client needs?
Maybe a curve all day through?
As you're going through that strategy or something to that effect.
Yeah, yeah.
So I think the big one is with City National Bank and that campaign again, the big curveball was the Department of Justice came down on them.
They were discriminating on their lending practices internally.
So there was a big crisis management effort that happened pretty much overnight where we tracked the SERPs, tracked AI, made sure their mentions were correct.
And then we went into immediate mode of, okay, we need a plan now because a part of that settlement was creating this educational library to meet the needs of the people.
So it was not only just getting found by people, but adhering to the regulatory regulations that they had signed and agreed to, to make sure they were compliant.

So that was probably the biggest thing that we had to act quick on where we wanted to have a good PR presence, try to get as much good press about this new loan grant, push out as much bad press about this DOJ settlement, which happened to be the largest redlining settlement.
So, I mean, you can imagine when DOJ.gov posts that online, it's like, okay, we need to get something going on here.
And even to this day, you know, it's still a continued tug of war because of that type of domain authority.
But yeah, so that was quick to act online.
had press releases ready if we needed to, based on traction that was gained, we immediately hit the ground running on content strategy and getting stuff out there.
And that was two years ago now or so.
So we've really continued that in the long term since we saw a great initial success.
So how does that work for you all?
You're doing both the strategy and executing the content as well.
Like, is that part of your internal team, essentially, who's building that content?
Yeah.
So the way that it works on this client specific, and we have different clients that work differently.
This one particular, we create, we identify opportunities via keyword research.
In the AI landscape, we're looking at the autofill when you go type in a few words on Google and see what people.

And then we go talk with ChatGPT about, hey, you know, if I ask this, what are 20 ideas that might be follow-up questions and try to get a better understanding of where these LLMs might be going with their answers.
We then create a product brief.
And by we, it's me on this account.
And that project brief says, okay, these are the key things we have to hit.
Here's the flow that somebody is most likely searching.
Here's who's currently ranking and what they're saying.
And then we look at embedding analysis with a tool called MarketBrew.
I think it's a pretty neat way to look at AI optimization.

It looks at, I know everybody talks about chunking, but it looks at it almost sentence by sentence and says, hey, this citation that is being pulled into the AIO or GPT summary is 90% identical to what's on page.
So we want to make sure we answer it with very similar words since they're close on the back end of how these LLMs are understanding these topics.
And we found really great success with that.
One of the case studies.
We share around was with McKinsey and Company.
They saw a 50% boost in AI citations on three articles.
So it was really successful.
But yeah, nonetheless, package that all up into a brief, tell the client which questions to answer.
That goes off to their writer, comes back to us for review.
And then once legal and compliance approved, we push live to the site.
Yeah, I got it.
So yeah, it sounds like you're running a lot of that end to end.
Is that like where you spend the vast majority of your time is on that customer essentially?
Yeah, that's the primary customer.
I mean, it changes every week though.
For instance, I talked about a project that was huge this morning that was on Delta Faucet.
So as a strategy director at Critical Mass, I can get pulled in any direction.
My allocations are certain.
They look a certain way, but they never seem to fall that way.
So on that team, it's totally different.
There's four strategists on that team that all kind of feed their work into me and I oversee their strategy and then help them out when things are not necessarily going the way we want.
In that realm, we're trying to get that client on board with more AI-optimized content because they have a real gap compared to their closest competitors.
But a lot of that project also lies in the paid search side, which I know isn't necessarily relevant to this role.
But I think as ChatGPT starts deploying paid ads in that space, it's always good to have an idea of how it's being integrated on Google and how it might look on GPT and whatnot in the future.
It's a good way to upsell clients if that's a service offering you guys offer in a year from now.
You never know, I suppose.
It's not something we've really talked a lot about.
It's a deep part of my experience.
And so sometimes our customers will ask me varying questions related to it and ask if we can help.
I'm like, theoretically, but not a core focus.
But who knows?
I think certainly some of the methodologies we take and just frameworks are applicable to paid in different ways.
So I don't know.
You can see that for sure.
How about on documenting processes?
Like, how do you approach that?
You know, walk me through maybe like an example of like a process that like you documented and developed.
And we'd love to hear a little bit more.
Yeah, love to templatize anything for the team.
So the way that I do this is I'll start with my, what I do for my process.
You know, I've been doing this a while, so I kind of have a flow.
So I'll build out what that looks like.
For example, in the AEO space, I like to first look at the AI overview, and I'll highlight that for the team.

Start here, step one, type in your target keyword, see what it says, highlight anything that you want to note, figure out who's being cited, notate that for later, and then continue to interact with the model.
And then I'll go to next step, next step, et cetera.
I like to package that all into like a Word doc.
So that's pretty straightforward to follow.
And then I'll package that up with
For the team, either in a live demo or a recording.
One of the things I established about three years or so ago at Critical Mass is called our in-house office hours for search strategists.

So we meet once a month, maybe once a quarter, depending on how busy it is and client needs, where it's myself or now other senior leaders are packaging up our templates, sharing them out to the team.
And then I'll manage that on the back end and keep housekeeping on where all the templates live, all these demos, et cetera.
But it's followed a really, really straightforward process where our new hires are able to hop in pretty quickly.
They have a folder all ready to go, and they just look at everything, and it's step one, step two, and it's easy to pretty much follow.
And you have a live demo, too, which helps a lot.
Yeah, for sure, for sure.
Do you all do anything to, like, go back and make sure some of that is up to date?
I feel like that's been one of our interesting challenges we have.
It's great to document, and it naturally is just a bunch of things change.
Is this, like, still relevant to the idea of some sort of, like...
So to try to flag that in some way.
Absolutely.
Yeah, it all has changed since AI has taken over our industry, as I'm sure you know.
So yeah, we do a pretty frequent audit of this.

And while the old documents don't necessarily get updated, we do like to keep them for legacy standpoint, because it's, I think, really helpful from a testing perspective to see what we were doing two years, a year prior, maybe six months ago now in the world of AI, because it moves so quickly.
So you can get an idea of where we were and where we're at today.
So we can look and learn from our, not mistakes, but maybe our trials and where they netted out.
So it's an interesting concept.
Instead of just getting rid of all of the stuff that exists, hey, let's build on top of it.
Let's revise it.
But for those that really like to dive deep in strategy and really in testing or A-B testing, I think it's great to have that legacy so they can see where we once were and what works.
Yeah, totally.
Makes sense.
Switching gears a little bit.
But so, you know, for us, like, I'm sure you see some version.
Yeah.
This, the technical aptitude of our clients can vary quite a bit.
You know, some who, you know, really don't understand SEO at any level to others that understand at a very deep level and get into the weeds.
I'd like generally our clients probably skew more to, they have some understanding, you know, obviously they hired us for a reason.
They understand that it's important to pay us, but, you know, far more price skewed, non-technical.
So I'm just curious, like, how do you typically think about strategy when you're communicating to those more like non-technical folks?
Yeah, that's one of the things I always determine on day one, whenever I meet the client for the first time.
I always like to start more simplistic than in the weeds.
And then I try to figure out who the key stakeholders are going to be client side that are either champions of SEO or have an idea of it or have either no idea of it.
And then frame up anything for them.
Like, for example, a lot of the VPs we work with or even C-suite.
And some of the clients that I'll present to, they know SEO as a channel.
They really have no idea what the hell we do, for lack of a better phrase.
They just get that it's a long-term value play and it gets people to the website.
So that's packaged up very, very differently where it just kind of explains, hey, as an overall strategy, we've established 100 articles in the last six months.
They're on the site.
Here's our movement.
Here's our conversions, et cetera.
It's moving your bottom line.
And then there's a core team that I'll work with that really understand SEO.
And then we start diving into rankings and how impression share has changed and what the competitors are doing.
So it really depends on the audience, but that's one of the core things I love to do, right?
When I meet a new client is say, hey, who are the people that I need to know where we go in depth with?
And who are the people that are going to be more that high level?
You kind of get it, but you really just care about, are you selling more of your product or your service?
Yeah, yeah, totally.
Makes sense.
Makes sense.
One last question for me and then I want to make sure I give you time for...
Questions as well.
So you were mentioning, you know, getting pulled into different clients, you know, classic agency stuff, you know, naturally going to happen, right?
How do you think about, like, prioritizing that, you know, you're naturally going to have points where there's going to be conflicting demands?
How do you generally, you know, especially if you had someone who is more new to agency set, how would you help coach them through that?
Yeah, definitely.
I think one of the key things that you have to figure out from the agency side is look at the agency's P&L.
And that's how you kind of focus on what clients you want to spend your time with when times get really tough and crunchy.
For example, if you sell in a million dollar project, you definitely want to spend time on that if you just sold that last week, and the other clients only paying you $150,000 that month.
So, you know, that's an easy way to look at it.
Where is the agency making the most money?
Or what is the newest relationship that you need to establish?
For example, there's been actually this week, I got pulled
This large presentation, like I was saying at the top of the call, I had this one client for four years.
They love me.
They have nothing bad to say.
And I was able to just say, Hey, look, I won't have time to get to this until the end of the week.
And they're totally cool with that because they've been established.
They know the quality of the work and have a good sense of it where the other clients knew and not too sure what to expect.
So that's how I spent that time.
And then depending on how senior the person is, if they're maybe a bit green to a senior role, I like to really work on delegating as much as you can.
I always like to think you want to train the people under you to replace your job one day.
It's always a good thing to be replaceable.
And it's something that everybody's got to be comfortable with, where if there is a junior that has, even if it's two hours of extra free time, Hey, let's go grab that.
And maybe they're not ready for the task at hand, but maybe they're 80% there and it's going to be better than having nothing ready.
And there's a lot of different ways we can juggle these asks when times get tough.
It just depends.
And open communication with the client at the end of the day is the biggest thing for me.
And especially if you have a great relationship with them, they tend to be very understanding.
And then you just have to hold to your word and get back to them when you say you will.
Yeah.
Yeah.
It's a great way to look at it.
Yeah.

Especially, you know, being a little bit more medium, long-term focused in that delegation at times, yeah, it might take more time up front, but hopefully you're building a lot of leverage for the future.
So very much resonates how we like to think here.
Easier said than done for sometimes us to do that.
I definitely have fallen into some traps where maybe don't, you know, lean into that as much as we can, but definitely like that philosophy.
What can I answer for you?
I think one of the things I found interesting was I was looking at your LinkedIn and it looks like you were at WPromote.
Well, you got acquired by WPromote at one point.
acquired.
Yeah.
It wasn't actually there.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Actually, the acquisition happened like soon after I left.
was like, yeah, a little while.
I like, they basically were that.
Yeah.
Place was known to be like, we were pure B2B SaaS and they struggled with getting B2B SaaS.
They're like, let's just buy a company that does that.
Well, hey, look at that.
I was going to ask what it was like coming from like a holding company to more of this startup world that you've now been in for a while.
But it almost sounds like you weren't necessarily at a holding company for, well, WPromo is privately owned, but still that type of enterprise.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
Definitely different style.

No, it you know, I heard more just through the grapevine because knowing a lot of people there, kind of like how that whole thing went down and this and that, but it was not a direct part of it in any way.
Yeah, not a problem.
Do you guys have people that have worked at the holding companies or enterprise level agencies that have come to GrowthX in the last two years or so and been like, what is their feedback?
What is their take?
Is it good, bad, the ugly, is it great, everything's smooth?
I'm sure we have.
trying to think of a specific person.
I would say generally we haven't leaned super heavy in terms of hiring agency people in the past.
Certainly, it's not that we don't have people with agency experience, but it hasn't been like a hard requirement.
I think one of the things we're coming back to is like it probably would be best to have people with a bit more agency experience.
You know, so that's one of the qualities we're certainly looking for now.
Yeah, if I honestly gave you anything here, I'd be making  up.
I don't think I have anything like legit that I've heard from an agency person outside of myself because, you know, my background being in agencies and then in-house.
I think I have by far the most agency experience here, if I'm not mistaken, but I could be forgetting someone off the top of my head.
All good.
All good.
I appreciate the transparency.
Yeah, not making an answer up.
I guess the other question is you obviously believe in the brand and the business and whatnot.
Where do you expect the company to be in two, three, five years?
Is it a successful exit?
Is it growing?
What is your vision?
Exit, you know, it's not like it obviously never crosses our mind, but there's just so many different directions, you know, that could go that it's hard to like really put too much stock into that.
Certainly, you know, see an opportunity for us to not just be this pure services play.
And, you know, we have the platform side of the business.
We're adding more and more alongside that.
And so there's different ways that could really take shape of like, how does that adapt?
What are, you know, how we function and operate into the future?
I think there's always going to be some services angle to what we do, especially like where AI is.
Currently, we're like, you know, certainly, you can't just automate everything, you can't just push a button and magic happens.

And, you know, you're going to have the, you're going to need to have the right inputs and thinking and strategy and the people to review those outputs and then continue to refine and build that system in the long AI.
When that happens, that that doesn't exist, is anyone's guess.
I mean, that kind of like opens up the big philosophical like AGI debate in some ways, right, to some degree.
And so, you know, I think our systems, our process, our people, all those different things will naturally get better and better over time.
So there will be things we do today that are more manual, that will be more automated.
But I don't, I don't know where that becomes like purely like there is not just some need for a service there in some meaningful way.
But certainly we see an opportunity.

That can go broader of like, you know, we've kind of framed this in different ways, but, you know, there's a lot of compelling things of like how a cursor can 10x the 10x engineers and all this and that.
And, you know, how you can, you know, find those bits of leverage for someone who, you know, is developing within, in a way that can be understood and that context can be built upon over time, right?
There's really not a lot of that for knowledge work.

And so, you know, if there is a, like a big, bold beyond what we're doing, that is certainly one that we have played around with in terms of our thinking of what if this can be a thing that's like cursor for knowledge work.
And, you know, you go through and you go through all the different bits of context of, okay, I'm developing this piece of content.
Who am I speaking to?
Why?
What are the?
The different edits and thinking that goes into that.
And how do you learn from all those inputs and outputs and how someone approaches that thinking?

And so, you know, the system that we're building certainly can, there can be a day in the not too distant future where we're starting to do that in more meaningful way just for purely like our customers and our content just from a pure content SEO output standpoint.
But there could be some paths where it broadens outside of that.
You know, that is a bit more like distant, distant of like, what could this develop into?
But I think in the more like short term where our, you know, focus is, like, it doesn't really feel like anyone has really taken the reins on the AEO front of things.
Naturally, there's still so much to figure out.
There's a lot of hot takes and this and that.
There's a lot of noise by these different, you know, profounds of the world and different.
Visibility monitoring tools, but none of them really are connecting the dots of like, okay, that's cool, but like, what are you actually doing?
And like, how are you actually actioning upon this?
And how does that create like a system of feedback of how you take that data that informs what comes next in a meaningful way?
And, you know, our main focus for the next, you know, foreseeable feature is really leaning into that.
How do we win on that space?
Because, I mean, it's just a clear shift.
All our, I'm sure you see it too, like our customers' boards are asking them about it.
The CEOs are asking about it.
Like, you know, sometimes some of that is just noise, but there's, it's clearly that there's a lot of time and attention to focus on that.
And so, you know, our, our next focus is like, okay, well, we believe no one has really put nearly as much time into trying.
All right.
I
To think through how do you take the benefits of AI and allow someone to move a lot faster in creating content?
How do you connect that with all the different signals that we can gather and experiment with with AEO?
And can that help someone move faster in that space where, you know, for some of our customers, you know, we're starting to see like literally overnight visibility into posting an article.
And so like if there's something meaningful to build off today, I mean, that potential insane rapid growth for for some of our customers.
I think the challenge, though, is like what actually is going to move the needle, you know, like we have a lot to do to to like really define our process.
I think generally I think generally our philosophy of like coming back to being persona focused and developing really meaningful experiences for them, first and foremost, helps lift most boats.
them to the that we
And whether it's a Google or a ChatGPT, there's some semblance of trying to reduce friction to someone getting a really meaningful, good answer.
And so that guides a lot of what we do.
But a lot of the around-the-edges optimizations or the more, hey, we've seen this work consistently countless times.
Sometimes we have some hypotheses, but all our time and attention needs to be going into shifting that.
No doubt there's more we can improve on how we do SEO deliverability.
And there's some overlapping aspects to what it looks like to do that at a high level and what it looks like to do AEO at a high level.
But I think if we just rely on that, we're going to be left behind in terms of where everything's shifting to.
So not like super direct to your answer, but like that really.
It's like where we have to put a lot of time and attention and focus, you know, happy customers, of course, retention, winning on AEO, on the delivery side, like all that's just so critical.
That makes sense.
Yeah.
And I mean, it's still so early in the environment that it's hard to know where we're going to be in six months, let alone three, two, three years from now.
Some of the stuff now is going to look like child's play that we're, you know, playing with now in six months, a year.
So, right.
I know we're at time.
you mind if I ask one more question?
Oh, yeah, for sure.
Yeah.
The last question I really have for you is, I know Jason gave me his vision of this role and what it looks like.
What is a successful placement for you guys, for you on this role?
What does that person do?
And in a year or two years from now, what do they look like at the agency in the future?
He's probably better suited, honestly, to answer that question.
Full transparency, have not, like, thought about it from that perspective yet.

I wouldn't be out of the, it wouldn't surprise, I mean, you know, we had, I don't know if anyone shared this with you, but we had a point earlier in the year where, you know, the early days of our life cycle, like our contracts were like three months, rolling three month contracts and you could opt out and, you know, it was easy and like the first handful of clients to like just land the initial clients.
But to show a bit of like repeatability momentum, we wanted to, how do we get to 12 month contracts?
And then, you know, we were fortunate enough to start landing like, you know, 100K plus 12 month deals, which is pretty rare for, you know, on this.
And, you know, it's doable, but not super common, more like SaaS level, you know, deals typically.
But how we got that into that initially was doing three month pilots.
And essentially it was like, you could opt out, you know, you'd have to give notice at like 60 days and you could get a contract.
And so what that meant is that just like a lot of folks, was like pretty low stakes for them to sign up.
But and it also meant like we were probably like not as dialed in on ICP and things of that nature.
So it helped us grow like really, really fast.
But also we were like some things like we maybe shouldn't have taken on some of these clients.
could have set our team up better for success and this and that.

They start with the eight week process with our team.
We have a dedicated strategy sprint.
Our essentially, our SoWs are now structured in two distinct phases.

And so how do you repeatedly execute across that, calibrate on that, if you will.
